reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The FeatureComparisonTable for load balancing is excellent — concrete cost figures, clear decision support, and covers both variants with equal depth including separate code examples for each.

Service discovery hostname is explained concretely in two places (basic example prose and the service-connect subsection): 'A service named `internalApi` is reachable at `http://internalapi:3000`'. No AWS jargon required.
The 'When NOT to use' section is genuinely opinionated and links precisely to alternatives (web service, worker service, Lambda, batch job) with clear one-line rationale for each.

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (hostname, connectTo), use-case/AWS (cold-start, gRPC, cost), and choosing/comparison (worker vs private, Lambda vs private). Answers are 2–4 sentences, citable, and concrete.

Health checks section correctly narrows scope to only `internalHealthCheck` (no ALB-level check for service-connect) rather than copying the web-service's two-check pattern verbatim — source-grounded differentiation.
Pricing context line is specific: distinguishes service-connect (~$0.50/month) from ALB (~$18/month) overhead on top of Fargate compute — gives the reader immediately actionable cost signal.
Code examples use focus markers, hidden imports, and realistic variable names (`internalApi`, `internalApi.ts`) consistent with the example page's convention.
Opening paragraph is 40–60 words and self-contained — names the resource, its defining constraint (no public endpoint), use cases, and what Stacktape automates.
problems: H2 ordering drift (mandatory): 'Packaging' appears after 'Side containers' (position 9) but the example places it before 'Connecting to other resources', 'Health checks', and 'Side containers' (position 9 in example vs. position 6-8 in draft). Shared section relative order must match the example.

H2 ordering drift (mandatory): 'Private subnets' appears after 'Remote sessions' (position 12) but the example places it before 'Logging' and 'Remote sessions' (position 15 vs. 16-18 in example). Correct order for these three is Private subnets → Logging → Remote sessions.

The Packaging section provides only a table with links — the example page adds a 'Using a custom Dockerfile' H3 with a working code example to give readers at least one non-buildpack path inline. Per equal-depth convention, the section is noticeably thinner than its counterpart on the example page.

The `protocol` property ('http' | 'http2' | 'grpc') is mentioned only inside the Logging section, which frames it as a logging concern when it actually controls protocol-specific CloudWatch metrics. The placement is confusing and the property is unique to private services — it deserves more prominent or at least better-situated treatment.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder H2 sections to match the example's shared-section ordering: 'Packaging' must appear before 'Connecting to other resources', 'Health checks', and 'Side containers'. The target order for the middle block is: Load balancing → Packaging → Connecting to other resources → Health checks → Side containers.

Reorder H2 sections to match the example's shared-section ordering: 'Private subnets' must appear before 'Logging', which must appear before 'Remote sessions'. Current draft reverses this to: Logging → Remote sessions → Private subnets.
optionalImprovements: Add at least one H3 subsection inside 'Packaging' with a code example — either 'Using a custom Dockerfile' or 'Using a prebuilt image' — so readers who can't follow a link still have a concrete starting point. The table alone matches the example's table but not its accompanying code depth.

Move or split out the `protocol` property explanation from inside the Logging section into a brief note in the Logging section intro or as a standalone sentence earlier (e.g., in the Basic example section or as its own one-paragraph H2 'Protocol metrics'). Its current location implies it only affects logging rather than CloudWatch metrics broadly.

The Compute resources section mentions EC2 (`instanceTypes`) in prose but gives no code example for it, while the example page also skips an EC2 example — this is consistent, but adding a brief 'when to prefer EC2 over Fargate' sentence (e.g., sustained high-throughput internal traffic, cost-optimized workloads) would match the example page's guidance depth.

The side containers section shows a `run-on-init` example but doesn't include an `always-running` example. The example page shows the same pattern but slightly undersells the always-running sidecar use case (log forwarders, monitoring agents) for internal services where this is common. A second minimal always-running example or an explicit 'when to use each' table would complete the section.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong decision-support for the load balancing choice: the FeatureComparisonTable and surrounding prose explicitly name cost, reachability scope, and the crossover condition — readers can decide without guessing.
Both load balancing variants (service-connect and application-load-balancer) receive equal treatment: a dedicated H3, a working code example, and a concrete tradeoff paragraph.

The Connecting to other resources section covers both directions — the private service connecting outward AND other services connecting inward via connectTo — which is the key pattern readers need for microservice wiring.
Pricing context is accurate and source-grounded (~$0.50/month service-connect, ~$18/month ALB, ~$9/month base Fargate task).

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (how to connect, hostname format), AWS use-case (cold-start, gRPC), and choosing/comparison (vs worker service, vs Lambda, vs web service). All 10 answers are 2-4 sentences with concrete numbers.
internalHealthCheck defaults (intervalSeconds 30, timeoutSeconds 5, retries 3) and ranges are accurate per the source and match the example page's depth.
The When NOT to use section is concrete with four distinct alternatives and appropriate links, not a generic disclaimer.
problems: H2 ORDERING DRIFT (mandatory): 'Packaging' and 'Connecting to other resources' appear in reversed order relative to the example. In the example, Packaging (position 9) comes before Connecting to other resources (position 10). In the draft, Connecting to other resources (position 6) comes before Packaging (position 9). Per example-guided mode rules, shared sections must preserve the example's relative order.

H2 ORDERING DRIFT (mandatory): 'Private subnets' is placed after Logging and Remote sessions in the draft (position 12). In the example, Private subnets (position 15) comes before Logging (16) and Remote sessions (18). The draft inverts this, placing Private subnets after both.

Packaging section lacks the deeper H3 code example that the example page provides ('Using a custom Dockerfile'). The draft shows only the table of five modes with no per-variant worked example, making it shallower than the example page at the same section.

No Alarms section. The example page includes an Alarms H2. Private services using application-load-balancer load balancing would have access to ALB alarms (error rate, unhealthy targets). The omission leaves readers who switch to ALB without guidance on monitoring.

The `protocol` property (http | http2 | grpc) is buried in the Logging section with a single line. It has no code example and no clear signal that it's a separate concern from logging — readers who deploy gRPC services may miss it.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder H2s to match the example's relative order for shared sections. Specifically: move 'Packaging' to appear before 'Connecting to other resources' (not after Side containers). Move 'Private subnets' to appear before 'Logging' and 'Remote sessions'. The corrected order for the middle sections should be: Load balancing → [any private-service-specific sections] → Packaging → Connecting to other resources → Health checks → Side containers → Private subnets → Logging → Remote sessions → FAQ → API Reference → Referenceable parameters.
optionalImprovements: Add an Alarms H2 section noting that when loadBalancing type is application-load-balancer, ALB alarms (error rate, unhealthy targets) are available, matching the example page's pattern.

Add at least one H3 code example inside the Packaging section (e.g., 'Using a custom Dockerfile') to match the example page's per-variant depth. The table alone covers enumeration but not decision-support.

Promote the `protocol` property (http | http2 | grpc) to its own sub-section or at minimum to the Load balancing section with a short code example, so gRPC users can find it when configuring their service — it is more of a networking/routing concern than a logging concern.

The basic example prose says containers are 'addressable from other containers in the stack by name' but doesn't mention that the name is lowercased — readers who name resources with mixed case (e.g., 'internalApi') may be confused when the hostname is 'internalapi'. Add a one-sentence clarification at the end of the basic example's follow-up paragraph.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first opening paragraph is self-contained and AEO-ready: ~55 words, names the resource explicitly, gives use cases, and describes what Stacktape handles.

FeatureComparisonTable for load balancing options is strong — both service-connect and application-load-balancer get equal depth: code examples, cost signal, when-to-use guidance, and concrete hostname/DNS context.

Complete 10-question FAQ spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connection, hostname, scale-to-zero), AWS use-case (cold-start, cost), and comparison (vs worker service, vs Lambda).
Pricing context is grounded in source files: ~$0.50/month for service-connect and ~$18/month for ALB are taken directly from the source JSDoc; Fargate compute cost table is accurate.
No forbidden patterns: no fenced YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig, no YAML alternative tabs.

Property values in code examples are explained in surrounding prose: cpu, memory, architecture, minInstances, maxInstances, scalingPolicy thresholds, and internalHealthCheck parameters are all anchored.
Packaging table covers all five container packaging modes with links to dedicated docs — avoids duplicating cross-cutting content while still enumerating all variants.
The 'When NOT to use' section is concrete and actionable, naming specific alternative resources (web service, worker service, Lambda, batch job) with reasons.
problems: H2 ordering drift (mandatory): Multiple shared H2 sections appear in a different order than the example. In the example the order is: Load balancing → Packaging → Connecting to other resources → Health checks → Side containers → Private subnets → Logging → Remote sessions. In the draft the order is: Load balancing → Connecting to other resources → Health checks → Side containers → Packaging → Logging → Remote sessions → Private subnets. Five sections are out of place relative to the example. Per the example-guided rules this is a mandatory fix and a scannability penalty.

H2 ordering drift impacts audienceFit: readers who move between sibling pages in the compute section will encounter a different conceptual flow in this page vs web-service.mdx, which erodes trust in the section as a whole.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder H2 sections to match the example page's relative ordering for shared sections. The correct sequence is: Load balancing → Packaging → Connecting to other resources → Health checks → Side containers → Private subnets → Logging → Remote sessions. Currently Packaging appears after Side containers (should be before), and Private subnets appears after Remote sessions and Logging (should be before both).
optionalImprovements: Add at least one code example under the Packaging section (e.g., custom Dockerfile or prebuilt image) to match the example page's depth, which includes a '### Using a custom Dockerfile' subsection with a working example.

In the Side containers section, add a note that an 'always-running' sidecar crash causes the entire task to fail — this is stated in the source type's JSDoc and is an important operational caveat readers need when deciding between container types.

In the 'Connecting to other resources' section, show the format of the injected STP_[NAME]_ADDRESS variable (e.g., 'http://internalapi:3000') so readers know exactly what the value looks like in their application code.

The Basic example's post-deploy note ('reachable at http://internalapi:3000') correctly explains the default address, but it could note that this hostname is only reachable from within the same ECS cluster/stack — not from Lambda functions unless ALB load balancing is used. This distinction is easy to miss and is the primary footgun for new users.

The Remote sessions section could mention that `stacktape container:session` is the specific CLI command to use (the web-service example mentions `stacktape container:session` in the same section and links to its CLI reference page), currently the draft links to the CLI page but does not spell out the command name inline.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-sourced and accurate. All scaling property names and defaults, compute resource properties, pricing calculations, service-connect hostname behavior, load balancing options, and connectTo env-var injection are verified against the TypeScript source. One minor missing-caveat was found: `logging.retentionDays` is a constrained union of discrete values but the draft implies free configurability. No invented routes, no incorrect property names, no wrong pricing figures, no fabricated Stacktape behavior.

issues.statement: "Retention is configurable via `logging.retentionDays`" implies an arbitrary numeric value is accepted.
issues.reason: The property is a constrained union type accepting only specific discrete values (1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 14 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 | 365 | 400 | 545 | 731 | 1827 | 3653). A reader who tries `retentionDays: 45` will get a TypeScript type error. The draft does not communicate this constraint.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a parenthetical note, for example: "Retention is configurable via `logging.retentionDays` (allowed values: 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365 days, and longer)."

positiveFindings: Scaling property names and defaults are all correct: `minInstances` (@default 1), `maxInstances` (@default 1), `scalingPolicy.keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder` (@default 80), `scalingPolicy.keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder` (@default 80) — all verified against ContainerWorkloadScaling and ContainerWorkloadScalingPolicy in multi-container-workloads.d.ts.

Compute resource property names are accurate: `cpu` (union 0.25|0.5|1|2|4|8|16), `memory`, `instanceTypes`, `enableWarmPool`, `architecture` ('x86_64'|'arm64', @default 'x86_64') — all present in ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig with matching types.
Fargate CPU-memory compatibility ranges in the pricing table (e.g. 0.25 vCPU → 512–2048 MB, 16 vCPU → 32768–122880 MB) exactly match the JSDoc combos table in ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig.

All pricing calculations in the Fargate cost table are arithmetically correct against the distilled pricing source (~$7.29/month per 0.25 vCPU, ~$1.60/month per 512 MB). The ~$9 minimum for 0.25 vCPU / 512 MB and ~$851 maximum for 16 vCPU / 122880 MB are accurate.

Load balancing type union (`service-connect` | `application-load-balancer`) correctly reflects PrivateServiceLoadBalancing source. The cost figures (~$0.50/mo and ~$18/mo) match JSDoc comments in private-services.d.ts.
Default port 3000 is confirmed by `@default 3000` on the `port` property in PrivateServiceProps.
`protocol` values (`http` | `http2` | `grpc`) match the source union type exactly.
`logging.disabled` (boolean, @default false) and `logging.retentionDays` (@default 90) property names and defaults are verified against ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging.

`connectTo` env-var injection pattern (PrivateService → ADDRESS → `STP_INTERNAL_API_ADDRESS` for resource `internalApi`) is consistent with the connectTo docs in __helpers.d.ts and the camelCase-to-SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE conversion shown in examples.
Service Connect hostname claim (lowercased resource name) is consistent with the commented-out `alias` JSDoc in PrivateServiceProps: 'By default alias is lowercased name of the resource.'

All cross-page links (`/resources/compute/web-service`, `/resources/compute/worker-service`, `/resources/compute/lambda-function`, `/resources/compute/batch-job`, `/resources/security/bastion-host`, `/configuration/connecting-resources`, `/packaging/overview`, `/observability/log-forwarding`, `/cli/container-session`, `/cli/debug-logs`) are valid routes in the navigation index.

`enableWarmPool` documented restriction ('Only works with a single instance type') matches the source JSDoc: 'Keep pre-initialized EC2 instances ready for faster scaling. Only works with a single instance type.'
NAT Gateway cost (~$32/month) and `stackConfig.vpc.nat.availabilityZones` (1|2|3) match the NatSettings interface and JSDoc in __helpers.d.ts.

ARM Graviton ~20% cost reduction claim is backed by both the ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig JSDoc ('arm64 is ~20% cheaper') and the distilled pricing source ('ARM (Graviton) tasks bill ~20% less than x86').
`minInstances` cannot be set to 0 is explicitly confirmed by ContainerWorkloadScaling JSDoc: 'Set to 0 is not supported — minimum is 1.'

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the private-service type, but it overstates several Stacktape-specific behaviors and includes a few configuration examples that are not supported by the provided TypeScript definitions. The biggest risks are unsupported hostname/address details, an invalid EC2 resources example gap, and claims about deployment/logging/metrics behavior that go beyond the source.

issues.statement: The draft says other containers can reach a private service at `http://internalapi:3000` or a lowercased resource-name hostname such as `myauthservice`.
issues.reason: The active source only says other containers can reach the private service “by name” and gives `http://myService:3000` as the example. The lowercased-alias text in `private-services.d.ts` is commented out, and the generic Service Connect type says the default alias is derived from resource and container names, not simply lowercased resource name. This could make readers hard-code an address that is not guaranteed by the documented type surface.
issues.suggestedFix: Avoid promising a lowercased hostname. Say other containers can reach a private service by name, and recommend using `connectTo` plus the injected `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_ADDRESS` value instead of hard-coding the hostname.

evidence.quote: Other containers in the same stack can reach it by name (e.g., `http://myService:3000`).
By default, the alias is derived from the resource and container names (e.g., `my-resource-my-container`).

issues.statement: The `Compute resources` section says Stacktape supports two compute engines and gives a Fargate code example, but EC2 only gets a short prose mention with no working example.
issues.reason: The page names both variants of the `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` concept, but only Fargate receives a concrete configuration example. The documentation-quality requirements call this unequal variant depth when a section covers multiple variants. The source confirms EC2 is a real supported variant, so the page should either provide comparable EC2 treatment or narrow the section to the default Fargate path and point readers to the API reference for EC2.
issues.suggestedFix: Add an EC2 resources example showing the actual `resources` shape from `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig`, with tradeoffs and the `instanceTypes`/`enableWarmPool` constraints grounded in source; or remove the EC2 subsection from the guide-level section and leave EC2 to the API reference.

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.

issues.statement: The opening paragraph says Stacktape handles “zero-downtime deployments automatically.”
issues.reason: The provided private-service and shared container sources describe packaging, scaling, health checks, logging, resources, and access, but they do not document zero-downtime deployment behavior for private services. This is a Stacktape-specific operational guarantee and should not be asserted without a deployment source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “zero-downtime deployments automatically,” or replace it with a sourced statement about the properties shown in `SimpleServiceContainer` such as health checks and scaling.

issues.statement: The side container example uses `new CustomDockerfilePackaging(...)` for a `sideContainers[].packaging` value.
issues.reason: The provided source defines container workload packaging variants as structured packaging objects such as `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackaging`, but it does not show a `CustomDockerfilePackaging` class or type. Elsewhere in the draft, container examples use `StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging`, which is also not visible in the provided `.d.ts` source as a class name. Since examples must be grounded in source files, this constructor name is unsupported by the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use only class names that are present in the provided class-based API sources, if supplied. If class API sources are not available, avoid showing side-container packaging constructors or use a simpler example that does not require an unsupported constructor name.

issues.statement: The draft says `connectTo` injects `STP_INTERNAL_API_ADDRESS`, and that with an ALB the injected address “reflects this ALB endpoint.”
issues.reason: The source confirms `PrivateService` exposes an `ADDRESS` referenceable parameter and that `connectTo` injects variables in the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern. It does not specify the exact normalization from `internalApi` to `INTERNAL_API`, nor does it say the value changes to an ALB endpoint when `loadBalancing.type` is `application-load-balancer`. The ALB endpoint detail is Stacktape-specific behavior not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded part: `connectTo` injects a private-service `ADDRESS` value using the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern. Do not state the exact env var spelling for camelCase names or the ALB-specific value unless a source confirms it.

evidence.quote: Environment variable names are in the format `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[VARIABLE_NAME]` (e.g., `STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`).
**`PrivateService`** → `ADDRESS`

issues.statement: The logging section says logs are retained for 90 days by default, retention is configurable via `logging.retentionDays`, and `logging.disabled: true` stops sending logs to CloudWatch.
issues.reason: The source supports automatic CloudWatch logging and 90-day retention, but the provided files do not show the shape of `ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging`, including `retentionDays` or `disabled`. Those property names may be correct elsewhere, but they are not grounded in the provided source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced CloudWatch/90-day statement and either remove the specific `logging.retentionDays` and `logging.disabled` property names or verify them against the `ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging` type source before publishing.

evidence.quote:
Container output (`stdout`/`stderr`) is automatically sent to CloudWatch and retained for 90 days.
   * View logs with `stacktape logs` or in the Stacktape Console.
logging?: ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging;

issues.statement: The `protocol` section says setting `protocol` enables protocol-specific CloudWatch metrics, including “request count metrics for gRPC servers.”
issues.reason: The private-service source says `protocol` is for metrics collection and gives HTTP 5xx tracking as an example. The Service Connect source says protocol-specific metrics can include HTTP 5xx errors. The provided sources do not specifically support gRPC request count metrics.
issues.suggestedFix: Say `protocol` enables protocol-specific metrics collection and use the sourced HTTP 5xx example. Avoid naming gRPC request count metrics unless a metrics source confirms that exact metric behavior.

evidence.quote: Protocol for metrics collection. Set to enable protocol-specific metrics (e.g., HTTP 5xx tracking).
Specifying the protocol allows AWS to capture protocol-specific metrics, such as the number of HTTP 5xx errors.

issues.statement: The draft says a private service cannot be exposed publicly later and that “there is no property on a private service that adds a public endpoint.”
issues.reason: The provided `PrivateServiceProps` source does not include a public endpoint property, so the narrow property claim is reasonable. However, the broader “not directly” guidance could be read as a lifecycle limitation, and the source does not document migration or conversion behavior. The safe, source-grounded wording is that `PrivateServiceProps` does not define a public endpoint configuration and users should choose `WebService` when they need a public HTTPS endpoint.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite as: “`PrivateServiceProps` does not include a public endpoint option. Use a web service when the container needs a public HTTPS endpoint.” Avoid claiming what can or cannot be changed later.

evidence.quote: loadBalancing?: PrivateServiceLoadBalancing;

positiveFindings: The draft uses the required `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript' ... }]} />` pattern for Stacktape configuration examples and does not hand-author YAML configuration examples.
The draft includes exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="PrivateServiceProps" />` and the definition name matches the provided `PrivateServiceProps` interface.
The pricing context for Fargate, Service Connect, Application Load Balancer, ARM discount, and NAT Gateway is broadly grounded in the provided type comments and generated pricing summary.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 Q&A pairs and uses only routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Load balancing section is the standout — the FeatureComparisonTable with cost rows and clear 'Reachable from any VPC resource' distinction directly answers the decision the reader faces, and the two subsections each get equal depth with a working example.

Both Fargate and EC2 are given separate H3 subsections with individual code examples and explicit when-to-use guidance, satisfying the per-variant equal-depth rule better than the example web-service page does.

connectTo coverage is unusually thorough: the section explains both outbound (what this service can call) and inbound (what other resources receive when they connect to it), including the ADDRESS env var pattern — exactly the information a first-time user needs.

FAQ spans all three required categories well. Stacktape-specific ('How do I connect from a web service?', 'Can I expose this publicly?'), AWS use-case ('How fast does Fargate cold-start?'), and comparison questions ('private service vs worker service', 'vs Lambda') are all present and substantive.
Pricing context is grounded in both the source JSDoc (~$0.50/month service-connect, ~$18/month ALB) and the Fargate pricing file, and is correctly qualified with '~' throughout.
Heading style, frontmatter shape, H2 ordering relative to the shared sections, and MDX component palette all match the example web-service page cleanly — no drift.
problems: Missing Alarms section — the example web-service page has a dedicated Alarms section, and per the source types (ApplicationLoadBalancerAlarm, ApplicationLoadBalancerErrorRateTrigger, ApplicationLoadBalancerUnhealthyTargetsTrigger), a private service running in application-load-balancer mode inherits ALB alarms. A user choosing ALB for broader VPC reachability gets no guidance on the alarm types that then become available.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Perfect answer-first paragraph: 56 words, self-contained, covers what + why + concrete use cases — extractable as-is by AI Overviews.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is factually solid. All Stacktape-specific property names, defaults, valid values, and pricing figures are confirmed against the source files. The pricing table math is verified against the distilled AWS pricing source. All seven CPU tiers and their memory ranges match the type definition exactly. Scaling policy defaults (80%), health check defaults and ranges, minInstances/maxInstances defaults, the 'architecture' and 'enableWarmPool' property names, and the load balancing type values are all confirmed in multi-container-workloads.d.ts. All cross-links resolve to valid routes in the navigation index. One meaningful missing caveat was found regarding always-running sidecar container failure behavior.

issues.statement: The Side containers section describes `always-running` sidecars as running 'for the entire lifecycle of the task' and being usable for 'log forwarders, monitoring agents, or sidecar proxies', but omits that if an always-running sidecar crashes, the entire task (including the main container) is terminated and replaced.
issues.reason: The source's JSDoc for ServiceHelperContainer.containerType explicitly states: 'always-running: Runs alongside the main container for its entire lifetime. If it crashes, the whole task fails.' This is an important operational constraint that affects how readers should design their sidecars — a flaky monitoring sidecar can take down the main application container. Omitting it gives a false sense of safety.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence after describing always-running: 'If an always-running sidecar crashes, ECS terminates the entire task — including the main container. Ensure always-running sidecars are stable; use a process supervisor or a resilient base image for anything that could exit unexpectedly.'

evidence.quote: - **`always-running`**: Runs alongside the main container for its entire lifetime. If it crashes, the whole task fails.

positiveFindings: Pricing table for all seven Fargate CPU tiers is mathematically verified: vCPU costs (~$7.29/month per 0.25 vCPU) and memory costs (~$1.60/month per 512 MB) from the pricing source correctly produce the stated monthly ranges.
All seven CPU values (0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 vCPU) and the corresponding memory ranges per tier are confirmed exactly against ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig in multi-container-workloads.d.ts.

Property names 'architecture' ('x86_64' | 'arm64', default 'x86_64'), 'enableWarmPool' (EC2 only), and 'instanceTypes' are all confirmed in ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig — the draft's code examples are correct.

Scaling defaults are source-verified: minInstances defaults to 1, maxInstances defaults to 1, keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder defaults to 80, keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder defaults to 80. The 'cannot be set to 0' constraint is confirmed ('Set to 0 is not supported — minimum is 1').

Health check defaults and ranges are confirmed in ContainerHealthCheck: intervalSeconds default 30 (range 5–300), timeoutSeconds default 5 (range 2–60), retries default 3 (range 1–10), startPeriodSeconds range 0–300.
Load balancing type values ('service-connect' and 'application-load-balancer') and the service-connect default are confirmed in PrivateServiceProps and PrivateServiceLoadBalancing.
All five container packaging modes (stacktape-image-buildpack, custom-dockerfile, prebuilt-image, nixpacks, external-buildpack) are correctly enumerated and linked to their dedicated packaging pages.

All cross-links resolve to valid routes in the documentation navigation index: /resources/compute/web-service, /resources/compute/worker-service, /resources/compute/lambda-function, /resources/compute/batch-job, /packaging/overview, /cli/debug-logs, /cli/container-session, /resources/security/bastion-host, /configuration/connecting-resources, /observability/log-forwarding.
The connectTo ADDRESS injection for PrivateService is confirmed: ScriptEnvProps and ResourceAccessProps both document that connecting to a PrivateService injects the ADDRESS parameter.
The 'EC2: First type in the list is preferred' claim is confirmed in the ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig instanceTypes JSDoc: 'First type in the list is preferred.'

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the PrivateService type and uses the required CodeBlock pattern, but several configuration-level claims go beyond the provided sources. The highest-risk problems are unsupported EC2 resource options, unsupported numeric defaults/limits, and unequal depth for side-container variants.

issues.statement: The EC2 example and prose claim `enableWarmPool: true` is supported, that it should be used with a single instance type, and that the cluster prefers the first `instanceTypes` entry when launching tasks.
issues.reason: The provided source only says EC2 resources use `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost. It does not document `enableWarmPool`, a single-instance-type requirement, or first-entry preference behavior. This could lead readers to copy a property or rely on behavior not grounded in the source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the warm-pool and first-instance-type claims unless a source file defining `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` supports them. Keep EC2 guidance to the sourced `instanceTypes` behavior, or add a sourced quote if available.

evidence.quote: * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.

issues.statement: The Side containers section documents both `run-on-init` and `always-running`, but only gives a working example for `run-on-init`.
issues.reason: The source defines two side-container variants. The draft gives one variant materially more depth than the other, which violates the per-variant equal-depth rule for polymorphic configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add an `always-running` example with comparable guidance and tradeoffs, or shorten the section to a linked overview without a variant-specific example.

evidence.quote: containerType: 'run-on-init' | 'always-running';

issues.statement: The Fargate section states exact valid CPU values, memory compatibility ranges, and that `architecture` defaults to `x86_64`.
issues.reason: The provided sources support that Fargate uses `cpu` and `memory`, and that ARM bills about 20% less than x86. They do not define the valid CPU set, CPU-to-memory compatibility matrix, an `architecture` property, or an architecture default.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep only the sourced pricing and high-level Fargate guidance, or add a source that defines `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` before documenting exact values and defaults.

evidence.quote: * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
- Prices are us-east-1 list price; ARM (Graviton) tasks bill ~20% less than x86.

issues.statement: The Scaling table states defaults and constraints for `minInstances`, `maxInstances`, and CPU/memory target utilization, including `minInstances` defaulting to 1 and being unable to be 0.
issues.reason: The provided sources document that scaling exists and that Fargate cannot scale to zero, but they do not define the `ContainerWorkloadScaling` shape, defaults, or numeric constraints shown in the table.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the default/constraint table or replace it with cautious prose unless the scaling type source is added and confirms those values.

evidence.quote:
* #### Auto-scaling: add/remove container instances based on demand.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Traffic is automatically distributed across all running containers.
- Total monthly cost = (vCPU rate × tasks × hours) + (memory rate × tasks × hours). Idle tasks still bill — Fargate cannot scale to zero.

issues.statement: The Health checks section states exact defaults and ranges for `intervalSeconds`, `timeoutSeconds`, `retries`, and `startPeriodSeconds`.
issues.reason: The provided source confirms `internalHealthCheck` exists and unhealthy containers are replaced, but it does not include the `ContainerHealthCheck` definition with the numeric defaults or ranges used in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the numeric defaults/ranges or add and cite the source file that defines `ContainerHealthCheck`.

evidence.quote:

   *
   * ---

issues.statement: The custom Dockerfile section says: “If omitted, Stacktape looks for a `Dockerfile` in the build context directory.”
issues.reason: The provided packaging source says `dockerfilePath` is optional and describes its path basis, but it does not document a default lookup behavior for omitted `dockerfilePath`. This is a default-value claim without source support.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the omitted-default sentence or phrase the example as explicitly setting `dockerfilePath`.

issues.statement: The Logging and FAQ sections say `protocol: 'grpc'` enables gRPC-specific CloudWatch request count metrics.
issues.reason: The source supports protocol-specific metrics generally and gives HTTP 5xx as an example. It does not specifically document gRPC request-count metrics, so the draft over-specifies what Stacktape exposes.
issues.suggestedFix: Say `protocol` enables protocol-specific metrics, and avoid naming gRPC request-count metrics unless a source confirms them.

evidence.quote: * #### Protocol for metrics collection. Set to enable protocol-specific metrics (e.g., HTTP 5xx tracking).

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph with a clear pricing context line immediately after — matches example page rhythm exactly.
FeatureComparisonTable for load balancing modes is well-structured with cost, reachability, and default comparisons — the most important decision axis for this resource.
Both compute engines (Fargate and EC2) are covered with separate code examples and tradeoffs, satisfying per-variant equal depth.
All five container packaging modes are enumerated in a table before deep-diving into the custom-Dockerfile example — union-type coverage is complete.
Both side container types (run-on-init and always-running) have dedicated H3 sections with examples and a Warning callout explaining what happens when an always-running sidecar crashes.

10-question FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo, scale-to-zero, gRPC), AWS service use-case (cost, cold-start), and choosing/comparison (vs worker service, vs Lambda).
Decision support is present for every opt-in feature: ALB vs service-connect includes cost and reachability tradeoffs; private subnets includes the NAT Gateway cost Warning callout.
All code examples are complete and copy-paste runnable — no placeholder values or ellipsis shortcuts.
connectTo section correctly explains both directions: outbound access to resources and inbound address injection when other resources connect to this service.
The <ApiReference> and <ReferenceableParams> components are present and correctly placed at the bottom, matching the example page's convention.
problems: Missing Fargate cost table: the example page has a detailed per-vCPU/memory-range cost table; this page has only inline prose cost figures, which are less scannable and harder to cite.

EC2 section's when-to-use guidance is vague ('concrete cost or capability requirements that Fargate can't meet') — no concrete scenario is given (e.g., GPU workloads, a specific instance family, sustained task counts where EC2 pricing wins).

The `protocol` property (top-level PrivateServiceProps) is introduced only at the end of the Logging section, implying it is a logging configuration option rather than a service-level property. Readers building gRPC services may miss it entirely.

H3 subsections inside Compute resources (### Fargate, ### EC2) deviate from the example page's flat prose approach for the same section, creating a minor structure inconsistency. The example presents both options under one H2 without H3s.

Alarms coverage is absent. While the source does not list private-service as a directly alarm-enabled resource, a private service using `application-load-balancer` load balancing would get ALB alarms — a parallel to the web service Alarms section that the example has. A brief note about this path is missing.
optionalImprovements: Add a Fargate cost table (columns: vCPU, memory range, approximate monthly cost) matching the example page's format. Source data is present in the pricing distillation file and the page already references these figures in prose.

Strengthen the EC2 when-to-use guidance with at least one concrete scenario — e.g., 'Switch to EC2 when you need a specific instance family (GPU, high-memory), when you have sustained workloads running hundreds of tasks and a reserved-instance discount matters, or when you need instance-level control Fargate doesn't expose.'

Introduce the `protocol` property earlier — ideally in the Basic example section or as a dedicated sub-point under Compute resources / Basic example, explaining it enables protocol-specific CloudWatch metrics. Current Logging placement can mislead readers into treating it as a logging setting.

Consider flattening the Compute resources H3s (### Fargate, ### EC2) to match the example page's flat prose structure. The content depth difference between Fargate and EC2 is more apparent with H3s; flat prose allows the sections to blend more naturally.

Add a brief Alarms note: when `loadBalancing.type` is `application-load-balancer`, the private service gets ALB alarms (error rate, unhealthy targets) via the underlying ALB — mirroring the web service's Alarms section and completing the feature parity story for ALB users.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-researched and largely accurate. Pricing numbers, load balancing type values, port defaults, connectTo variable names, side-container lifecycle rules, NAT costs, and all cross-links are correctly grounded in source. Two issues merit attention: the container buildpack language listing omits PHP, .NET, and Ruby which appear in the type union, and the `architecture` property constraint (ignored when `instanceTypes` is set) is not surfaced for EC2 users. All other claims verified against the provided source files are correct.

issues.statement: The packaging table row for Stacktape image buildpack says 'Zero-config, works with JS/TS, Python, Java, Go' and the FAQ answer says 'The Stacktape image buildpack supports Node.js, Python, Java, and Go gRPC servers.' Both omit PHP, .NET, and Ruby.
issues.reason: The `StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps` type (which extends `StpBuildpackSharedProps`) inherits a `languageSpecificConfig` union that includes `PhpLanguageSpecificConfig`, `DotnetLanguageSpecificConfig`, and `RubyLanguageSpecificConfig` alongside JS/TS, Python, Java, and Go. The type definition (highest-priority source) lists more languages than the JSDoc summary and the draft acknowledges.
issues.suggestedFix: Update the packaging table row and the FAQ answer to include PHP, .NET, and Ruby. Example: 'Zero-config, works with JS/TS, Python, Java, Go, PHP, Ruby, and .NET'.

`protocol` values ('http', 'http2', 'grpc') exactly match the source union `protocol?: 'http' | 'http2' | 'grpc'`.
`enableRemoteSessions` default (false) and `container:session` CLI reference are correctly sourced from `SimpleServiceContainer.enableRemoteSessions?: boolean; @default false`.
`usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT` default (false) and behavior description match `SimpleServiceContainer.usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT?: boolean; @default false`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided private-service types and pricing sources. The main issues are a few unsupported Stacktape-specific details and one opt-in networking section that needs more decision support.

issues.statement: "If omitted, Stacktape looks for a `Dockerfile` in the build context directory."
issues.reason: The provided type source only says `dockerfilePath` is optional and relative to `buildContextPath`; it does not document a default lookup path or fallback behavior. Because this affects whether readers can omit the property safely, the draft should not state the default unless a higher-priority runtime source supports it.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the default-lookup sentence, or narrow it to the sourced behavior: "`dockerfilePath` is relative to `buildContextPath`; set it when the Dockerfile is not in the location you want Stacktape to build from."

issues.statement: "Use `connectTo` on any calling resource to have Stacktape inject the service address automatically" and "Any resource that `connectTo`s this service receives the `ADDRESS` parameter injected as `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_ADDRESS`."
issues.reason: The source supports `PrivateService` exposing an `ADDRESS` referenceable parameter through `connectTo`, but "any calling resource" is broader than the scoped set of resources shown in the source. Readers could infer that every Stacktape resource, including routing or infrastructure resources, accepts `connectTo`.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace "any calling resource" with "any workload or script/resource type that supports `connectTo`" and keep the link to the connecting-resources page for the complete list.
